result.markdown:
--- title: "Phase 4 — Inter-Individual Variability (IIV)" subtitle: "Adding random effects on CL, V, and Ka" author: "Pramod BR" date: today format: html: theme: cosmo toc: true toc-depth: 3 code-fold: true number-sections: true fig-width: 8 fig-height: 6 execute: echo: true warning: false message: false --- ## Objectives The base model (Phase 3) allowed only **clearance** to vary between subjects. Here we add **between-subject random effects** on **V** and **Ka** as well, so each subject has their own clearance, volume, *and* absorption rate. We then test whether the extra variability is justified — better fit, well-estimated, and plausibly distributed. ## What a random effect is For each parameter we write, e.g., `cl = exp(tcl + eta.cl)`, where `eta.cl` is a subject-specific deviation drawn from a normal distribution `N(0, omega^2)`. The population shares one typical curve; each subject's curve is a shifted version of it. The spread of those shifts — the IIV — is summarised as a **CV%**. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} print(iiv_model) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` function () { ini({ tka <- log(1.5) tcl <- log(2.8) tv <- log(32) eta.ka ~ 0.3 eta.cl ~ 0.1 eta.v ~ 0.1 add.sd <- 0.2 prop.sd <- 0.2 }) model({ ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka) cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl) v <- exp(tv + eta.v) d/dt(depot) <- -ka * depot d/dt(center) <- ka * depot - (cl/v) * center cp <- center/v cp ~ add(add.sd) + prop(prop.sd) }) } ``` ::: ::: ## Fit the IIV model ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dat <- build_nonmem_data() base_fit <- readRDS(fs::path(PATH_MODELS, "base_fit.rds")) iiv_fit <- fit_iiv_model(dat) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` calculating covariance matrix done ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} iiv_fit ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} \begin{align*} {ka} & = \exp\left({tka}+{eta.ka}\right) \\ {cl} & = \exp\left({tcl}+{eta.cl}\right) \\ {v} & = \exp\left({tv}+{eta.v}\right) \\ \frac{d \: depot}{dt} & = -{ka} {\times} {depot} \\ \frac{d \: center}{dt} & = {ka} {\times} {depot}-\left(\frac{{cl}}{{v}}\right) {\times} {center} \\ {cp} & = \frac{{center}}{{v}} \\ {cp} & \sim add({add.sd})+prop({prop.sd}) \end{align*} ::: ::: ## Parameter estimates ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} base_param_table(iiv_fit) |> kbl(caption = "IIV model estimates (fixed effects, IIV CV%, and shrinkage)", digits = 3) |> kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
IIV model estimates (fixed effects, IIV CV%, and shrinkage)
Parameter Est. SE %RSE Back-transformed(95%CI) BSV(CV%) Shrink(SD)%
tka 0.389 0.198 50.9 1.48 (1, 2.17) 68.8 1.88%<
tcl 1.03 0.074 7.22 2.79 (2.41, 3.22) 25.7 1.12%<
tv 3.47 0.046 1.33 32 (29.3, 35) 12.6 15.7%<
add.sd 0.261 0.261
prop.sd 0.136 0.136
````` ::: ::: Read the **BSV(CV%)** column for the size of each random effect and the **Shrink(SD)%** column for how well the data support it. As a rule of thumb, shrinkage under ~30% means the individual estimates are genuinely informed by the data rather than collapsing to the population mean. ## Is the added variability justified? We compare the base and IIV models by objective function value (**OFV**), and by **AIC/BIC**, which penalise extra parameters. By the likelihood-ratio test, adding two random effects (V and Ka) is significant at p < 0.05 if the OFV drops by more than **5.99** (χ², 2 df). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cmp <- compare_models( model_metrics(base_fit, "Base (IIV on CL)"), model_metrics(iiv_fit, "IIV on CL, V, Ka") ) cmp |> kbl(caption = "Base vs IIV model comparison", digits = 2) |> kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Base vs IIV model comparison
Model OFV AIC BIC nPar dOFV dAIC
Base (IIV on CL) 177.81 432.41 449.70 6 0.00 0.00
IIV on CL, V, Ka 104.36 362.96 386.02 8 -73.45 -69.45
````` ::: ::: The IIV model lowers the OFV by **73.45** points relative to base (ΔAIC = -69.45). A drop well beyond the 5.99 threshold means the between-subject variability on V and Ka is **statistically warranted** — the model genuinely fits better, not just by added flexibility. ## Random-effect distributions The empirical-Bayes η estimates should be centred on 0 and roughly symmetric — the model's normality assumption. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} iiv_plot_eta(iiv_fit) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Eta distributions for CL, V, and Ka (centred on zero = assumption met).](04_IIV_Model_Report_files/figure-html/eta-plot-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Key takeaways - Random effects on **V and Ka** were added to the base (CL-only) model. - The OFV fell by **73.45** (≫ the 5.99 cut-off for 2 df), so the extra IIV is **justified**. - Shrinkage and the η distributions indicate the random effects are data-supported, not artefacts. - This full-IIV model is now the reference onto which we test a **covariate** (body weight) next. > Next: **Phase 5 — Covariate model**, testing whether body weight explains part of > the between-subject variability in CL and V. ```
